name: Sourdough Bread

ingredients.item: For the crust, coarse cornmeal or sesame seeds are

method: Autolyse: In a large mixing basin, blend flours and water until
thoroughly mixed using your hands. Cover and leave away for
at least 1 hour, up to 3 hours in a warm section of your kitchen,
ideally 78°.
Combine salt and beginning in the dough with your hands and
stir until thoroughly mixed. Continue to knead the dough by
hand, lifting it from the bottom up and letting it fall over itself
until it becomes less sticky and can be picked up in one
drooping piece, about 3 to 4 minutes. (If the bread is still too
sloppy, rest it for at least 10 minutes before kneading it for
another 2 to 3 minutes.)
Bulk fermentation: This will take 4 to 6 hours to complete.
Cover dough and put it aside for 30 minutes in a warm area of
your kitchen. After 30 minutes, do your first set of stretch and
folds: carefully scoop half of the dough from one side and
remove it away from the bowl with moist hands, then fold it
over the remaining dough on the opposite side. Firmly but
gently raise the dough until you feel the tension in it, but not
until it rips. Turn the bowl a few degrees and continue,
stretching and folding the dough around all four edges. It should
now be in better form than it was when you started. Turn the
dough over gently to allow the seams to settle beneath. Cover
and set aside for 30 minutes to allow flavors to meld.
Stretching and folding should be done every 30 to 60 minutes.
The dough will get tighter as the gluten develops. If the dough is
too close, be careful and do not force a fold. Instead, allow it to
rest for a more extended period, up to an hour, before folding
again. Repeat the stretch and fold process at least four times
more until your dough feels like it can retain its shape.
After the final round of stretch and folds, cover and let the
dough rest for at least 30 minutes, up to 2 hours. After the bulk
fermentation time, the dough should have risen between 20%
and 50% in volume.
Pre-shape: Remove the dough from the bowl gently and place it
on a lightly floured board. The smooth side of the dough should
be facing up. Using a thin dusting of flour, gently dust the
dough. Pull dough towards you with one floured hand and a
bench scraper while rotating dough against the surface to
generate tension across the top of the dough. Repeat this
procedure until your dough is smooth, plump, and spherical.
Allow for a 10-minute rest period, uncovered.
Form: Gently turn the dough over, so the smooth side is facing
down. Lift the bottom side of the dough, extend it a couple of
inches toward you, and fold over most of the way towards the
top side of the dough with floured hands, as if wrapping a
present. Next, lift the dough on the left side, cross it over to the
rightmost of the way, lift the dough on the right side, and cross
it to the left. Finally, raise the top edge of the dough and fold it
to the bottom of the "package," closing it. Turn the bread
package over so that the seams are facing down, pull the dough
towards you, and spin a few times to generate additional tension
throughout the loaf, then set aside for 2 minutes uncovered.
Flour your banneton basket or a colander lined with a clean,
lint-free tea towel for cold proofing. (To ensure the dough
releases, use more flour than you think you'll need! Before
baking, you may always brush off any excess.) Reach under the
dough with your bench scraper and carefully turn the smooth
side into your free hand, then transfer the dough to the dusted
basket, smooth side down. Sprinkle the dough with flour and put
it in a plastic bag with a tight seal. Allow 15 minutes at room
temperature before transferring to the fridge for 12 to 16 hours.
ON DAY 2
Preheat the oven to 500 degrees and place a Dutch oven in the
center. Remove the pot from the oven with long oven mitts once
warmed and spread a liberal coating of cornmeal or sesame
seeds over the bottom of the pool.
Place your dough in the saucepan upside down, with the smooth
side facing up. Brush away any excess flour with a pastry brush.
Make one or more incisions across the dough with a razor, sharp
knife, or a pair of scissors to allow for expansion during the
baking.
Bake for 20 minutes with a well-fitting oven-safe cover on the
Dutch oven. Reduce the oven temperature to 475°F and bake for
another 10 minutes, covered. Remove the paper from the Dutch
oven and bake for another 25 minutes, or until the bread has
become a deep caramel brown color. (The internal temperature
of the bread should be 208 degrees.)
Cool: Allow 5 to 10 minutes for the inside of the pot to cool
before transferring to a cooling rack. Before slicing, let the
bread rest for at least 1 hour (ideally 2 hours).
